task: I need to write up a clean incident summary for a network problem that already got fixed, but I want it to hold up if someone pushes back later. What should I include so the timeline, evidence, and final cause are clear without making it longer than it needs to be?

turns.agent_response:
Use a tight structure that makes the **timeline, proof, and cause** easy to defend later.

## Incident summary template

### 1) Header
- **Incident title**
- **Date / start time / end time**
- **Scope**: site, VLAN, core segment, service(s) affected
- **Severity / impact**
- **Owner / responders**

### 2) What happened
One short paragraph:
- What broke
- Who/what was affected
- How it was detected
- Whether it was intermittent or total loss

### 3) Timeline
Keep this as an ordered list with timestamps:
- **T0** – first symptom observed
- **T+X** – alerts/logs/counters confirmed issue
- **T+X** – checks performed
- **T+X** – root cause isolated
- **T+X** – fix applied
- **T+X** – service verified stable

Use only facts. Avoid commentary inside the timeline.

### 4) Evidence
Include the minimum proof needed to support the conclusion:
- **Interface counters**: CRC/FCS, runts/giants, drops, discards, late collisions, input/output errors, pause frames
- **Event logs** around first symptom: interface resets, STP changes, LACP churn, SFP events, power events, watchdogs
- **Before/after comparison** if something was changed
- **Traffic proof** that service returned and stayed healthy
- **Timing proof** that the link or path was stable long enough to trust it

If relevant, note:
- what was **not** seen
- what was ruled out
- why you did **not** touch higher-layer services yet

### 5) Root cause
State it plainly:
- **Direct cause**
- **Contributing factors**
- **Trigger**
- **Why it caused the outage**

Example format:
- Direct cause: faulty optic on uplink A
- Trigger: increasing CRC/FCS errors led to link instability
- Contributing factor: redundant path also degraded, causing failover churn

### 6) Resolution
- What was changed
- By whom
- When
- Whether rollback was needed
- Whether the fix was temporary or permanent

### 7) Validation
Show how you proved recovery:
- no flapping
- no rising errors
- no loss of signal
- traffic passing normally
- stable for the required observation window

### 8) Follow-up actions
Short list:
- permanent fix
- monitoring added
- replacement scheduled
- config/process change
- owner and due date

---

## Keep it short by following these rules
- Use **facts, timestamps, and counters**, not narrative.
- Don’t include every troubleshooting step; include only the steps that **changed the conclusion**.
- Separate **symptom**, **evidence**, **cause**, and **fix**.
- If there was a change window or last known good state, mention it early.
- Don’t claim recovery until you have **proof of stability** and **actual traffic**.

---

## One-line example
“On 2026-04-28 from 09:14–09:42 UTC, users on Site A lost connectivity due to repeated uplink instability caused by a failing optic on core switch SW1; the issue was confirmed by rising CRC/FCS and interface resets in the event log, resolved by replacing the optic, and verified stable after 15 minutes with no flaps, no new errors, and normal traffic flow.”

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page incident report template** you can paste into a ticket or postmortem.

user_simulator.text:
Pretty much the right shape. But I’d tighten it more and make it harder to argue with later.

What I’d include, bare minimum:
- exact start time / end time
- affected site, device, interface, service
- first symptom seen and by who/what
- the specific evidence that rules in the cause, not just a pile of logs
- the fix applied
- validation window after the fix
- any remaining risk / follow-up if there is one

What I’d leave out:
- long troubleshooting narrative
- every dead-end check
- generic filler like “users were impacted” unless you can quantify it

Best format if you want it defensible:
1. Summary line
2. Timeline with timestamps
3. Evidence
4. Root cause
5. Resolution
6. Verification
7. Follow-up

For the timeline, keep it to the moments that changed the conclusion. If a step didn’t narrow the problem or prove anything, don’t bloat the record with it.

For evidence, I’d separate:
- symptom evidence: alerts, packet loss, flaps, errors
- isolation evidence: what you tested and what stayed clean
- cause evidence: the thing that actually failed
- recovery evidence: post-fix stability, counters flat, traffic normal

Root cause should be one sentence if possible. No “probably,” no vague chain of blame. If it was a bad optic, bad config, failed PSU, whatever — say that directly.

If you want, I can turn this into a tighter fill-in template with fields only, no explanation.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s the tighter version I’d use if you want it to be **short, defensible, and hard to argue with later**.

## Minimal incident summary structure

### 1) Summary line
One sentence:
- **What failed**
- **Where**
- **When**
- **Final cause**
- **How it was fixed**

Example:
> On 2026-04-28, connectivity for Site A’s core uplink failed due to a defective optic on SW1 Gi1/0/24; service was restored after optic replacement and verified stable for 15 minutes.

### 2) Timeline
Only include the moments that changed the conclusion.

Format:
- **HH:MM** — first symptom observed by X
- **HH:MM** — key evidence confirmed
- **HH:MM** — cause isolated
- **HH:MM** — fix applied
- **HH:MM–HH:MM** — validation window passed

Keep it factual. No commentary, no dead ends.

### 3) Evidence
Use only the proof that supports the final cause.

Split it into 3 bullets:

- **Symptom evidence:** alerts, flaps, packet loss, user reports, counters increasing
- **Cause evidence:** the specific log/counter/test result that rules in the failure
- **Recovery evidence:** counters stopped rising, link stayed up, traffic passed normally

If you can, include the exact metric or log line, not just “logs showed an issue.”

### 4) Root cause
One sentence, direct and specific.

Good:
- “Repeated CRC/FCS errors and link resets on SW1 Gi1/0/24 were caused by a failing optic.”

Avoid:
- “Likely hardware issue”
- “Some network instability”
- “Probably the uplink”

### 5) Resolution
Just state:
- what was changed
- by whom
- when

Example:
> Replaced optic on SW1 Gi1/0/24 at 10:22 UTC.

### 6) Verification
Show how you proved it stayed fixed:
- no flapping
- no new errors
- traffic normal
- stable for X minutes

If there’s a standard observation window, use it.

### 7) Follow-up
Only include if there’s real residual risk:
- replacement pending
- monitoring added
- config cleanup
- vendor ticket open

If there’s no follow-up, say:
> No further action required.

---

## What to leave out
- long troubleshooting narrative
- every test you ran
- generic impact language unless quantified
- speculation
- “probably,” “likely,” “appears to be”

---

## Best short-form template

> **Summary:** [What failed], at [site/device/interface], from [start] to [end], caused by [root cause], resolved by [fix], verified stable for [window].  
>
> **Timeline:**  
> - [time] first symptom observed  
> - [time] evidence confirmed  
> - [time] cause isolated  
> - [time] fix applied  
> - [time] validation passed  
>
> **Evidence:**  
> - Symptom: [fact]  
> - Cause: [fact]  
> - Recovery: [fact]  
>
> **Root cause:** [one sentence]  
>
> **Resolution:** [what changed]  
>
> **Verification:** [how stability was proven]  
>
> **Follow-up:** [if any]

If you want, I can turn this into a **copy-paste incident report form** with blank fields only.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. That’s the version I’d actually use.

It’s tight, it separates the parts that matter, and it doesn’t waste space on theater. The key thing is you’ve got the defensible sequence:
- symptom
- evidence
- cause
- fix
- verification

That’s what holds up when somebody starts poking at it later.

The only thing I’d maybe add, if it matters, is a field for **source of detection** — monitoring, user report, NOC, onsite tech, whatever. Not always needed, but if someone argues about when the incident really started, that helps.

Otherwise it’s good. Clean enough to copy into a ticket or postmortem without turning into a novel.
